source_paper_title: Primal-Dual Extrapolation Methods for Monotone Inclusions Under Local Lipschitz Continuity
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Lu_2025, title={Primal-Dual Extrapolation Methods for Monotone Inclusions Under Local Lipschitz Continuity}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0407}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0407}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Lu, Zhaosong and Mei, Sanyou}, year={2025}, month=nov, pages={2577–2599} }
context:
Section 7 (Concluding remarks), page 23

One natural question is whether the aforementioned operation complexity of \(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) can be improved to \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\), which would match the optimal complexity for solving non-strongly monotone inclusion problems under global Lipschitz continuity.
title: Remove the log factor in residual complexity for locally Lipschitz monotone inclusions
background_latex:
Let \(F:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n\) be a single-valued monotone operator and let \(B:\mathbb{R}^n\rightrightarrows\mathbb{R}^n\) be a maximal monotone (set-valued) operator with nonempty domain \(\mathrm{dom}\,B\). Consider the monotone inclusion problem
\[
\text{find }x\in\mathbb{R}^n\text{ such that }0\in (F+B)(x).
\]
Assume a solution exists. For \(x\in \mathrm{dom}\,B\), define the residual of \(F+B\) at \(x\) by
\[
\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x):=\inf\{\|v\|: v\in (F+B)(x)\}.
\]
A point \(x\in \mathrm{dom}\,B\) is an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution if \(\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x)\le \varepsilon\).
Assume further that \(F\) is locally Lipschitz continuous on \(\overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\): for every compact \(\Omega\subseteq \overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\) there exists \(L_\Omega>0\) such that \(\|F(x)-F(y)\|\le L_\Omega\|x-y\|\) for all \(x,y\in\Omega\).
An algorithm is measured in operation complexity by the number of fundamental operations, consisting of evaluations of \(F\) and evaluations of the resolvent \((I+\gamma B)^{-1}\) (assumed exactly computable) for \(\gamma>0\). Under global Lipschitz continuity of \(F\), the optimal complexity for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution of a monotone (not strongly monotone) inclusion is \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) in this operation model.
problem_definition_latex: Design an algorithm for the above monotone inclusion setting (monotone \(F\), maximal monotone \(B\), and \(F\) locally Lipschitz continuous on \(\overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\)) that finds an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution \(x\in\mathrm{dom}\,B\) with \(\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x)\le \varepsilon\) using at most \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) evaluations of \(F\) and \((I+\gamma B)^{-1}\), i.e., improve the known \(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) operation complexity to \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) in this local-Lipschitz setting.
latex:
Let \(F:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n\) be a single-valued monotone operator and let \(B:\mathbb{R}^n\rightrightarrows\mathbb{R}^n\) be a maximal monotone (set-valued) operator with nonempty domain \(\mathrm{dom}\,B\). Consider the monotone inclusion problem
\[
\text{find }x\in\mathbb{R}^n\text{ such that }0\in (F+B)(x).
\]
Assume a solution exists. For \(x\in \mathrm{dom}\,B\), define the residual of \(F+B\) at \(x\) by
\[
\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x):=\inf\{\|v\|: v\in (F+B)(x)\}.
\]
A point \(x\in \mathrm{dom}\,B\) is an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution if \(\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x)\le \varepsilon\).
Assume further that \(F\) is locally Lipschitz continuous on \(\overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\): for every compact \(\Omega\subseteq \overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\) there exists \(L_\Omega>0\) such that \(\|F(x)-F(y)\|\le L_\Omega\|x-y\|\) for all \(x,y\in\Omega\).
An algorithm is measured in operation complexity by the number of fundamental operations, consisting of evaluations of \(F\) and evaluations of the resolvent \((I+\gamma B)^{-1}\) (assumed exactly computable) for \(\gamma>0\). Under global Lipschitz continuity of \(F\), the optimal complexity for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution of a monotone (not strongly monotone) inclusion is \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) in this operation model.

Design an algorithm for the above monotone inclusion setting (monotone \(F\), maximal monotone \(B\), and \(F\) locally Lipschitz continuous on \(\overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\)) that finds an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution \(x\in\mathrm{dom}\,B\) with \(\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x)\le \varepsilon\) using at most \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) evaluations of \(F\) and \((I+\gamma B)^{-1}\), i.e., improve the known \(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) operation complexity to \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) in this local-Lipschitz setting.
open_problem_quote: One natural question is whether the aforementioned operation complexity of \(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) can be improved to \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\), which would match the optimal complexity for solving non-strongly monotone inclusion problems under global Lipschitz continuity.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for an improved worst-case upper bound (or matching lower bound) over a broad class of problems with only local Lipschitz continuity, which is not naturally falsified by searching finite instances. While one could numerically probe worst-case behavior on parameterized examples, that does not constitute a rigorous counterexample to a universal complexity claim.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize broad families of first-order splitting schemes with adaptive stepsizes/backtracking and use a computable fitness signal such as empirical iteration/operation counts to reach a target residual over a benchmark suite of monotone inclusions. This could discover candidate methods or heuristics that empirically remove the log factor, but translating empirical performance into a proven worst-case \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) guarantee remains nontrivial.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
